claims.claim: We investigate whether a lightweight personalization signal derived from each author's historical per-file line deltas can improve next-commit changed-file prediction relative to a non-personalized repository-wide file frequency and recency baseline.

evidence_refs.quote: # File-Delta Personalization Tuning: Author-Specific Edit History as a Next-Changed-File Ranking Signal

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Using chronological train/validation/test splits across four Git repositories (258 pooled held-out test events), author-personalized features improved pooled MRR by +0.067 (95% bootstrap CI [0.030, 0.104]), hit@5 by +0.050 (95% CI [0.012, 0.089]), and recall@5 by +0.057 (95% CI [0.029, 0.087]) over the baseline.

claims.claim: However, results are not uniform across repositories: one of four repositories exhibited MRR regression (−0.034) despite concurrent gains in hit@5 and recall@5, indicating that personalization can reorder rankings in ways that harm first-hit position even when top-5 retrieval improves.
